text.title: Vendor Evaluation and Anti-Patterns
text.summary: Weighted scoring approach, sensitivity analysis rule, and common evaluation failures.

rule.statement: Run a sensitivity analysis after scoring: re-score with each criterion weight shifted ±10%. If rank order flips, the choice is not robust — re-evaluate or document the trade-off explicitly.
rule.rationale: A rank that changes under small weight variations means the decision is essentially arbitrary within the measured range; false precision hides this.

example.note: Five criteria: Technical Capability 25%, Relevant Experience 20%, Price 25%, Timeline 15%, Team Quality 10%, References 5%. Vendor A weighted score 86.5, Vendor B 84.5. Sensitivity: swapping price weight to 35% changes winner to Vendor B. Decision documented: technical quality prioritized over cost for this engagement.

antipattern.description: Lowest price wins without quality and risk weighting.
antipattern.reason: Price-only selection consistently underestimates ramp-up cost, quality gap, and vendor management overhead; total cost of engagement typically exceeds a higher-priced reliable vendor.

antipattern.description: Reference checks summarized from the vendor's own proposal.
antipattern.reason: Self-reported references are marketing; independent reference checks require contacting previous clients directly, not reading testimonials in the bid document.

antipattern.description: Vague SOW accepted to start faster.
antipattern.reason: Every undefined deliverable becomes a dispute; a clear SOW is the single most important document in procurement — spending one week on it saves months of rework.

antipattern.description: No change process defined in the contract.
antipattern.reason: Scope always evolves; without a written change process, every addition is either rejected or silently absorbed until someone notices the cost overrun.
